When I provision the user and group information from the Public Cloud to the Work Zone via IPS, I got an error: The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  There is even an KBA addressing this problem 3505749 - The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  It turns out not because of my ProviderID is bad, but I only copied part of the ProviderID 6d5e30cb-4 from sap.start.6d5e30cb-4.  I learned that from creating my own ProviderID.  The entire string is the ProviderID, not part of it.  The details count.

Joule Icon Not Shown (4/9/2026)

While installing Joule recently, I found the Joule Icon won't show on the Fiori Launchpad. By further investigation, I learned that from Release 2602, SP 06, Patch 0005, a new authorization is implemented: for Joule icon to be seen, a user needs a business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC.  Here is the background information:

After running Joule Booster, two communication scenarios were created automatically:

  • SAP_COM_0647 for Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP Integration
  • SAP_COM_0882 for Joule Integration

Within SAP_COM_0882, there is one property called Plug-in Activation State. This property controls how the Joule icon shows up or not, or who can use Joule or not.  It has three available values:

  • Empty - Joule is available to all users;
  • DISABLED - Joule is not available to users;
  • ROLE - Joule is only available to those users with the proper authorization, i.e., the users with the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC.

Property Plug-in Activation State

 With this new parameter, the Administrator can control who can access Joule or not.

The business catalog is part of the SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE business role template. 

If you use a business role copied from the SAP's role template SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE , you need to re-sync it to add this business catalog.

If you don't use this SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE template, just create a new business role to assign the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC to it. You can name this new business role like "Display Joule Icon", and assign all users to it.

There is an KBA discussing this topic: 3720220 - SAP Joule Icon Does Not Appear in the SAP S4 HANA Cloud Public Edition After Configuration Has Been Completed. For the Public Cloud users, it is recommended to select the value ROLE so that you have control the access to Joule.

 

References

  1. Blog 1: Joule – End-to-End Setup Guide (for all Line of Business) Unified Approach
  2. Blog 2: Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Setup Guide
  3. Discovery Center 1: Establish a Unified Joule Instance
  4. Discovery Center 2: Activate Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  5. SAP Help: Joule onboarding guide
  6. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part A Prepara...
  7. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part B Joule I...
  8. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part C Joule D...
  9. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part D Improvements)

entry.title: Terraform in Practice: Setting Up Joule Studio
entry.content:
Introduction 

As Developer Advocates, we run SAP CodeJam events where participants need hands-on access to the Joule Studio environment, typically provided by us during the day of the event. The challenge: a Joule Studio tenant has limits on the number of deployments that can be made, and these limits are reached in a CodeJam setting where 30+ developers are simultaneously testing their skills and agents, pushing deployments etc, so each CodeJam event requires spinning up a brand new tenant from scratch knowing which subscription, instances, plan, role collections, destinations, and credentials to use while creating a new tenant. 

Doing these tasks by hand is a long and tedious process. It takes too many clicks and careful typing, which makes it easy to make a mistake. With CodeJam events happening frequently, this process was becoming a real bottleneck. Terraform seemed like a good choice at this point to perform these steps. Instead of repeating the same manual steps every time, we could automate the entire tenant provisioning process, turning what used to take significant manual effort into a few commands and getting a Joule Studio tenant at the end of the execution which typically takes 20 to 30 mins. 

I had the opportunity to explore Terraform during a hands-on session at SAP Inside Track, Bangalore in June 2025.  After trying it out in the developer hands on area, I could see its value for automating the creation and management of SAP BTP subaccounts. During Devtoberfest 2025, SAP experts on Terraform presented a session titled From Clicks to Code: Automate SAP BTP Setup with Terraform on how to set up a BTP environment with Terraform. While trying to learn more about Terraform, I also came across a learning.sap.com course that is comphrensive. The course is available here and the GitHub repository used in this learning course is available here. There are also some excellent posts in the SAP Community portal for beginners looking to get started with Terraform, the links to those posts can be found in the Additional References section. Rather than repeating what's already well-documented, I'll focus on the key concepts behind the Terraform and share a few things I picked up along the way and provide you a Terraform script to get started with Joule Studio setup.  

What is Terraform? 

Terraform uses HashiCorp Configuration Language for building infrastructure with configuration files. Terraform's configuration language is declarative, meaning that it describes the desired end state for your infrastructure. It also keeps track of your real infrastructure in a state file, which acts as a source of truth for your environment. Terraform uses the state file to determine the changes to make to your infrastructure so that it will match your configuration. Terraform builds a resource graph to determine resource dependencies and creates or modifies non-dependent resources in parallel. This allows Terraform to provision resources efficiently. Terraform plugins called providers lets Terraform interact with cloud platforms and other services via their application programming interfaces (APIs). SAP provides dedicated Terraform providers for BTP, Cloud Identity, Cloud Foundry, and Cloud Connector. The BTP provider functions by wrapping BTP CLI commands, allowing you to convert imperative CLI commands into declarative Terraform resources. See the registry for documentation on how to use them: https://registry.terraform.io/search/providers?namespace=SAP. 

Using Terraform as an Administrator 

There are two ways to run the terraform script to create a Joule Studio environment. We will look at how to run the Terraform scripts on a local machine with Terraform CLI first. The approach mentioned below requires minor changes depending on whether we are running it on a Mac or Windows machine. 

  1. Clone this repository https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform
  2. Open it in VS Code
  3. Go through the pre-requisites section in the README file 
  4. Install Terraform CLI https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/tutorials/aws-get-started/install-cli 
  5. Set up environment variables by running these commands on your VS Code terminal  

On Mac/Linux 

export TF_VAR_btp_username='your-btp-username' 
export TF_VAR_btp_password='your-btp-user-password'

On Windows (PowerShell) 

$env:TF_VAR_btp_username = 'your-btp-username' 
$env:TF_VAR_btp_password = 'your-btp-user-password'

6. Configure the values for global account subdomain, identity provider domain, subdomain name and region in the terraform.tfvars file

Project in VS Code 7. Go to the root directory and run the commands from your terminal one by one. 

terraform init
terraform apply --auto-approve

The terraform init command installs the SAP BTP and Cloud foundry providers configured in the main.tf file in the repository.  

The terraform apply command installs the entitlements and subscriptions mentioned in the .tf files. 

8. Once all the resources are created, run Joule booster to create formation to complete the Joule studio setup. See the steps here on running the Joule booster. 

9. Navigate to the Instances and Subscriptions page of your new BTP subaccount; ensure the following subscriptions and service instances have been successfully provisioned.

The second approach is explained in the GitHub Actions section. 

GitHub Actions 

Now, the local Terraform CLI approach works perfectly fine and well suited for a development workflow, but what if you would rather not install anything on your machine at all? 

That's where GitHub Actions comes in. GitHub Actions lets you automate anything, triggered either by an event in your repository (like a push or a PR) or kicked off manually straight from the GitHub UI. Your workflows live as YAML files inside the .github/workflows directory of your repository. 

Check out the README of this repository to follow this approach for creating your Joule Studio setup: https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform 

One thing worth knowing: you can override any terraform.tfvars variables defined in your repository with the corresponding values in the GitHub variables by passing them via the --var flag. You can see how to pass the variables in the terraform.yml file under .github/workflows of the repository. Anything you haven't explicitly overridden will simply fall back to whatever is defined in terraform.tfvars. 

Use this approach when you have a stable version of a script that can be used to create a new tenant with all the resources and dependencies. 

Using Terraform as a Developer 

My recommendation would be to stick to the local Terraform CLI approach while you are actively writing or updating your scripts. The reason is simple: when something fails during development, Terraform lets you fix just the broken parts and apply only those changes. That kind of control is needed when you are iterating quickly. 

Before you start customizing the script to fit your needs, there are also a handful of expressions and constructs worth getting accustomed to.  So, let’s walk through the significant ones. 

Variables 

All the variables used in the script are defined in the variables.tf file with an optional default value if needed. Configurable values such as global account name, sub account region etc can be provided in the terraform.tfvars file. Sensitive information like passwords and secrets should be stored as environment variables in local machines or as GitHub secrets in GitHub. Terraform makes this easy with the TF_VAR_ prefix. Any environment variable with that prefix gets automatically picked up as a Terraform input variable. So, if you set TF_VAR_btp_password in your environment, Terraform maps it directly to var.btp_password in your script. This keeps credentials in GitHub Secrets or Environment variables and passes them securely to Terraform without exposing them in logs or files. 

If a variable has no default value and you haven't provided it through .tfvars or an environment variable, Terraform will prompt you for it in the terminal during plan or apply. It won't silently fail, but it will interrupt your flow, so it's worth getting your variables sorted before you run anything.  

Built -in functions: 

There is a set of built-in functions available that you can use within your scripts from this reference. Some of the functions used in the repository are lower(), replace(), trim(), toset(), contains() etc. When a subaccount is created, the name of the subaccount subdomain needs to confirm certain rules defined by BTP. We could use these built-in functions to sanitize the user input provided in the terraform.tfvars file and create a subaccount subdomain that confirms those naming conventions.  

raw_subdomain = lower("${var.subaccount_name}-${var.subaccount_subdomain}-${var.subaccount_region}") 
subaccount_subdomain = trim (replace (replace (raw_subdomain, "/[^a-z0-9-]/", "-"), "/--+/", "-"), "-")

This code sanitizes and formats a string to create a subdomain by concatenating three variables into lowercase and using regular expressions to replace any invalid special characters or double-hyphens with a single dash.

Meta-argument 

depends_on is a meta-argument to define explicit dependency. For eg: if you want to create a subscription for Build Work Zone but the entitlement for Build Work Zone should exist before the subscription happens. In such cases, the depends_on argument can be used to ensure that the entitlement is created before the subscription. By default, Terraform executes resources in parallel.

local values block  

When you want to define a local variable or derive a value from a variable, you can use locals. For instance, the cf_api_url is derived from subaccount_region variable, so we can define it within local value block and then use them by referencing it as local.cf_api.url  

locals {
  cf_api_url = https://api.cf.${var.subaccount_region}.hana.ondemand.com 
}

Ternary operators 

Ternary operators can be used to apply conditional expressions. 

plan_name = var.environment == "prod" ? "standard" : "free"

If the environment is production, it assigns the value "standard" to plan_name.

What I learned about the Development workflow 

 After working with Terraform, here is what I consider to be the best practices to follow: 

  • Always start with terraform fmt -recursive before committing anything. It ensures your code style remains consistent and identifies basic syntax issues early, preventing them from causing problems later
  • Run terraform validate after every code change. It's quick, doesn't need credentials, and gives you instant feedback on syntax and data type errors 
  • Never skip the terraform plan -out=tfplan even for "small" changes. This step shows you exactly what Terraform is about to create, modify, or destroy. Review the output carefully before moving on
  • Then apply with terraform apply tfplan using the saved plan file. This guarantees you are applying exactly what you reviewed 
  • Avoid --auto-approve flags in production
  • Never commit .tfstate files as state files can contain sensitive data like passwords and secrets. Add this file to .gitignore. Use a secure remote state backend instead 
  • Always commit .terraform.lock.hcl. This locks your provider versions and ensures everyone on the team gets reproducible results

Further extension of the script 

Think of the script as your starting point, it gets you a working Joule Studio setup quickly. Depending on your scenario, you will likely want to extend it.  

For Joule to connect to SAP AI Core (for document grounding), an S3 bucket (for storing documents), an MCP Server (for access to live data) or external OData APIs via BTP destinations, you will need to add those destinations in Terraform. Create a new destination.tf file, add your destination configurations and commit it to the repository and execute the terraform cli commands.

One other thing worth mentioning is Cloud Identity Services. The script assumes your global account already has Cloud Identity Services set up, which is the case for most existing BTP accounts. If that is your case, all you need to do is provide the identity provider domain value in terraform.tfvars and you are good to go. 

However, if your account doesn't have Cloud Identity Services configured yet in the global account, you will need to add a resource for the entitlement and subscription as well. For that, I would point you to the Cloud Identity Services provider documentation, it covers exactly what you need to set that up. 

 Refer to the samples shown here for setting up destination and Cloud Identity Services

  •  https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/btp/latest/docs/resources/subaccount_destination 
  • https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/sap-cloud-identity-services/latest/docs 

 Additional References

  • https://github.com/SAP-samples/btp-terraform-samples 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/automating-sap-btp-setup-with-the-new-terraform-provider-for-sap-btp/ba-p/13549469 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/extending-infrastructure-as-code-for-sap-btp-managing-resources-on-sap-btp/ba-p/14062455 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/easy-way-to-automate-and-configure-the-setup-of-a-btp-account-and-cloud/ba-p/13677419

entry.title: Integrate SAP SuccessFactors and SuccessFactors Learning seamlessly with SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

I’m sharing this blog after extensive effort, research, and hands-on analysis. I hope it helps anyone looking to integrate SAP SuccessFactors and SuccessFactors Learning (LMS) while leveraging a custom domain through SAP Build Work Zone.

 

Requirement

When accessing the SAP SuccessFactors system, SAP provides a standard URL like:

https://hcm22.sapsf.com/login?company=<company name>

The SF Home page will look like as below:

 

 

After logging in, users are directed to the SuccessFactors dashboard as above. From there, navigating to the Learning Management System (LMS) opens another SAP-hosted URL:

https://<company name>.lms.hr.cloud.sap/learning/user/deeplink.do?fromSF=Y&linkId=HOME_PAGE

 

Challenge

Both URLs use SAP’s default hostnames. However, based on client requirements, access should be through a custom domain, such as:

https://hrhub.abcservices.abc.com

 

Solution Overview

To achieve this, we integrate SAP SuccessFactors with SAP Build Work Zone, where the Custom Domain Service is already configured.

For a detailed, end-to-end guide on Custom Domain Service, refer to my other blog:
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/custom-domain-service-in-sap-btp-build-work-zone-standard-edition/ba-p/14314511

 

Note: This setup uses SAP Build Work Zone – Standard Edition, but the same approach also works with the Advanced Edition.

 

Analysis & Implementation Steps

After thorough analysis, the following approach worked successfully:

Step 1: Create Destination in SAP BTP

Create a destination in the BTP subaccount pointing to the SuccessFactors system:

Destination Name: hrhub

 

Step 2: Configure Work Zone Content

In SAP Build Work Zone, create:

  • A local application which will be having the destination as "hrhub" and the App UI Technology will be Dynamic URL and Relative Path to App will be /sf/home

    here, we can also add one more app which we can show in the work zone and access the above app by following:

     

  • Page: Below will be the page where we added the App

  • Space: Add the page in the space:

     

  • Role: Add the space and app in the role so it will be visible to user:

     

     

Step 3: Assign Roles

  • Assign the role to the Work Zone site

     

  • Assign the role to users in the BTP subaccount

     

Step 4: Access via Custom Domain

You can now access SuccessFactors directly using the custom domain:

https://hrhub.abcservices.abc.com/site/workzone-std-prd?sap-ushell-config=headerless#sf-display

 

As per the above Screen shot, the SF Home page is opening through work zone URL and Dashboard is similar which we have seen when accessing directly through SF.

Now we can access the LMS by clicking on tile "View My Learning" or from the top Menu "Learning"

In the above URL, it is not changing the URL when accessing the LMS, the URL is remaining same as custom domain url which show the customer host instead of SAP Standard URL.

Common Issue & Fix

Issue

While navigating between SuccessFactors and LMS, you may encounter the following browser error:

SecurityError: Failed to read a named property 'document' from 'Window':
Blocked a frame with origin "https://<company name>-dev.lms.hr.cloud.sap"
from accessing a cross-origin frame.

Refused to display 'https://<company name>-dev.lms.hr.cloud.sap/'
in a frame because it set 'X-Frame-Options' to 'sameorigin'.

The above error will come first time when accessing the LMS, if we refresh the page then this error might go. In our case this error was coming when accessing first time and after refresh the page it was not coming. Below is the error screen shot:

 

 

Resolution of the above Error:

Solution 1: Apply the following SAP Note in LMS:

SAP Note 2169861 –
"Learning Plan Internet Explorer error 'This content cannot be displayed in a frame' or blank page in Chrome or Firefox"

Configuration Change in LMS Side

Navigate to:

System Admin > Configuration > System Configuration > WEB_SECURITY

Set the following property:

clickjackingProtection.enabled=false

⚠️Important: Disabling clickjacking protection should be evaluated carefully based on your organization’s security policies.

 

If the Solution 1 is not acceptable in your Organization Security policy then we have one more solution which is suggested by SAP Team and not having any security concern.

Solution 2: Please use the following config entry in the exception list
clickjackingProtection.excludeURI[landOnPortalHome.do].uri=/user/personal/landOnPortalHome.do

The above Solution 2 is not having any security concern and it will also fix the issue.

Summary

This guide outlines the key steps to integrate SAP SuccessFactors and SuccessFactors Learning with SAP Build Work Zone using a custom domain. This approach enhances user experience by providing a seamless and branded access point while maintaining centralized navigation.

I hope this helps streamline your implementation and reduces dependency on standard SAP URLs.

If you have any questions or run into issues, feel free to reach out.

Regards,
Rohit Gera

entry.title: What's New in SAP BTP - Q1 2026 Innobytes
entry.content:
This first quarter has set the tone for 2026, bringing in new ideas and innovations all around Data, Joule and more. Let’s dive into some of the highlights Q1 has brought us.

 

 

AI & Data Foundation Highlight

Discovery and Data Agent in SAP HANA Cloud

The new Discovery and Data Agents are lowering learning barriers for developers and data users who just need answers. These new agents make it easier to explore, analyze, and act on data without needing deep knowledge of the underlying artifacts.

To see this demo in action, watch the video (starting at 0:11).

Learn more

  • Discovery & Data Agent on SAP Community
  • Register for the SAP HANA Cloud Data & Discovery Agents - Beta Program

 

Integration Highlight

Fresh Connections in SAP Integration Suite

New adapters for SAP Integration Suite on SAP BTP help transform your integration landscape, breaking down data silos, streamlining operations, and allow rapid adoption of new capabilities to innovate faster. 

The latest release offers:

  • The BambooHR adapter enables receiving and processing employee lifecycle data effectively.
  • The Microsoft Outlook Sender and Receiver adapter allows seamless integration for calendars, mail, attachments, and messaging directly into your mission-critical workflows. 

For Edge Integration Cella:

  • The File adapter, enabling secure file exchange with NFS systems
  • The OFTP2 adapter for compliant B2B connections to securely exchange messages from business partners. 

See the new integration content (starting at 2:14).

Learn more:

  • Integration Adapter
  • SAP Integration Suite on SAP Community

 

Agent & Application Development Highlights

API based Action Error Handling

In real-world automation, failures are inevitable. What matters is how the system handles them. This innovation brings precision and predictability to API error handling in SAP Build Process Automation on SAP BTP. When configuring an API-based Action step, enable error handling and define specific errors, handling authentication failures precisely. You can create generic categories, such as 4XX or 5XX, handling broader client or server issues.

The inability to define category overlap eliminates ambiguity, and deterministic runtime behavior is guaranteed. When an error occurs, precedence is strictly enforced:

  • Specific errors first
  • Generic category second
  • Undefined fallback last

At the Action level, you can configure retries that define the number of attempts and the delay between them. Afterwards, the error handling logic is executed. Every stage is fully logged in Monitoring, ensuring complete traceability and turning error handling from a black box into a controlled, observable workflow decision.

Watch the demo (starting at 3:35).

 

Conversational Work in SAP Build Work Zone 

There is now a powerful new way to work in SAP Build Work Zone. Save time by simply asking Joule what you need instead of clicking through multiple pages.

In the demo (starting at 5:33), a new project workspace for a cross-functional initiative is set up through a short conversation with Joule, enabling easy navigation, no technical steps, and only one screen. SAP Build Work Zone becomes more conversational, more intuitive, and more aligned with how people actually work.

Learn more:

  • Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

 

Core Platform Highlight

SAP Automation Pilot and Joule: Unlocking AI-Powered Ops Agents

The SAP Automation Pilot and Joule turn repeatable operational tasks into intelligent, agent-driven capabilities on SAP BTP.

By starting with a real operational process like a S/4HANA health check, use Automation Pilot’s low-code/no-code interface. Here, the necessary steps, inputs and validations can be collected and grouped into a Model Context Protocol (MCP) Server. The MCP Server exposes these automations as governed, reusable tools that can be safely consumed by AI agents without writing custom APIs.

Once published, connect it to Joule Studio to build a Joule Ops Agent that understands when and how to invoke these tools. The Ops Agent can now detect issues or fetch system status, triggering the right automation behind the scenes.

The DevOps or Admin team can simply ask the Ops Agent in Joule to ‘check my HANA Cloud and App instances health and remediate common issues’ by using the MCP Server to execute these steps securely, consistently, and audibly.

For a demo, watch the video (starting at 7:35).

Learn more:

  • Blog Post: From Automation to Agentic Ops
  • Blog Post: Meet your Ops Agents

 

Share your thoughts 

These videos are for YOU, and we would love to hear your impressions. How do you like the video? How can we improve upcoming videos? Please share your feedback by liking this post or leaving a comment below.

Thank you for your support and happy viewing, sharing, and enjoying!

You Might Also Be Interested In

  • SAP BTP Garage: monthly LIVE webinar with hands-on use case walkthroughs and demos.
  • Unlocking SAP BTP: monthly podcast covering all things SAP BTP.

entry.title: Managing Content Change Notification Issues During SAP Fiori Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP
entry.content:
With the SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure, you can integrate launchpad content from a product into SAP BTP by using an automatic synchronization. During the automatic synchronization, it may happen that the exposure log receives the following error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” (Message Class: /UI2/CDM3_EXP, Message Number: 399).

As you may not immediately recognize that this issue happened, this blog post provides a way of how you can monitor this type of error message using email notification from SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (SAP Cloud ALM).

In short, in the SAP Cloud ALM Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you do the following:

  1. Enable the ABAP application log for the system          
  2. Add a filter for the error message
  3. Add a notification for the error

As an example, we use an SAP S/4HANA system.

Disclaimer: This article is written from the viewpoint of a consumer of SAP Cloud ALM. If you have SAP Cloud ALM-specific questions, such as how to set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM, please reach out to SAP Cloud ALM directly.

Check the following SAP Help Portal pages to see whether you are affected by this issue:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP Integrated Business Planning

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP S/4HANA on-premise

  • Manage Content Change Notifications
  • Launchpad Content Exposure Troubleshooting - Error Analysis   

Prerequisites

Before we can start with the parts specific to launchpad exposure in the SAP Cloud ALM configuration, you set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM.

In addition, please ensure that you have the Integration Architect role. See SAP Cloud ALM overview page.

Enabling ABAP Application Log for Your System

First, you configure your system to collect data for the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In SAP Cloud ALM, in the Operations space, open the Integration & Exception Monitoring app.
  2. Now, let’s add a system tile to the overview: In the upper right corner, choose the Select a Scope icon and filter for your system. Select it in the table and choose Apply.
  3. On the overview page, choose the system tile and from its menu, choose Edit Configuration.
  4. For your system, toggle the Data Collection to On.
  5. For your system, choose the arrow icon to go to the details page.
  6. Activate the ABAP Application Log entry.

Adding a Filter for the Launchpad Content Exposure Error Message

Now, let’s create a filter so that we can monitor when the error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” is written to the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In the same screen as above, for the ABAP Application Log, enter the monitoring and events view.
  2. To add the filter, in the Filters table, choose Add.
  3. Enter a filter name. Then, enter the following filter parameters:

    Parameter

    Operator

    Value

    Object

    Is

    /UI2/BE

    Subobject

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3

    Message ID

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3_EXP

    Message Number

    Is

    399

    Status

    Is

    E


  4. Save the filter by choosing the Save button at the bottom of the dialog.

Adding an Event to Be Notified About an Error

The last step is to create an event so that you get notified once the filter which we have created in the previous step detects the launchpad content exposure error message.

Please note that this event will not only be initiated for the launchpad content exposure error message. If you had configured other ABAP Application Log filters in addition, the event will be initiated as well. The reason is that on the date of publication of this blog post, according to 3452860 - How to setup filters for the category "ABAP application log" in Exception Monitoring of SAP Cloud ALM, it is not possible to add filters to the event itself.

  1. To create the event, navigate to the Events tab and choose Add.
  2. As Event Name, choose Erroneous ABAP Application Log and enter a display name.
  3. Define the actions: Activate Create Alert. For example, you can also configure the event to send an email once there is an error written into the ABAP Application Log.
     
  4. Save your entries.

Checking the Event

In the Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you can view all events that you have created and that were initiated on the Alerting tab.

  1. Choose the Alerting tab.
  2. Once the launchpad content exposure error happens, you can see it in the table

Conclusion:

This blog post covered how to proactively monitor HTTP 401 content change notification failures during SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure to SAP BTP using SAP Cloud ALM's Integration & Exception Monitoring app. You have learned how to enable ABAP Application Log data collection, create a targeted filter for message /UI2/CDM3_EXP 399, and configure automated email notifications to detect synchronization issues before they impact your users. By implementing this monitoring approach, you can transform reactive troubleshooting into proactive incident management, ensuring continuous content availability between your on-premise systems and SAP BTP.

If you have questions about the configuration steps or would like to share your experiences with launchpad content exposure monitoring, please feel free to leave a comment below.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-1]
entry.content:
Developing the CAP Application Locally with SQLite

In this blog series, I will walk you through building an Incident Management application using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) with Node.js, connected to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment.

The frontend is a Fiori Elements application deployed separately to the HTML5 Application Repository — making this a fully decoupled architecture.

Here is what we will cover across this series:

— Develop the CAP application locally using SQLite (this post)
— Connect the CAP app to SAP HANA Cloud
— Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry
— Build the Fiori Elements UI using SAP Fiori tools
— Configuring Destination in BTP
— Deploy the Fiori app to the HTML5 Application Repository

Before deploying anything to the cloud, it is always a good practice to build and test your CAP application locally. In this part, we will:

- Initialize the CAP project
- Define the domain model (entities)
- Create two OData services (Admin and Processor)
- Add sample data using CSV files
- Run and test the application locally using SQLite as the database

Let's get started!


PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed and configured on your machine before proceeding:

- Node.js (v18 or higher recommended)
- @sap/cds-dk installed globally:

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk

- VS Code with the SAP CDS Language Support extension (recommended) / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Basic familiarity with CAP and OData concepts is helpful


STEP 1 — CREATE THE CAP PROJECT

Open a terminal and run the following command to initialize a new CAP project with Node.js support:

cds init --add nodejs incident-management

This creates a new folder called "incident-management" with the standard CAP project structure.

Navigate into the project folder:

cd incident-management

Install the required Node.js dependencies:

npm install

 

After installation, your project structure will look like this:

incident-management/
├── db/ → Domain models (entities, types)
├── srv/ → Service definitions (OData services)
├── app/ → UI applications (used in later parts)
├── package.json
└── .cdsrc.json


STEP 2 — ADD THE DOMAIN MODEL

The domain model defines all the data entities for our application.
Inside the "db" folder, create a new file named: schema.cds

Paste the following code into schema.cds:

using {
  cuid,
  managed,
  sap.common.CodeList
} from '@sap/cds/common';

namespace sap.capire.incidents;

/**
* Incidents created by Customers.
*/
entity Incidents : cuid, managed {
  customer     : Association to Customers;
  title        : String @title: 'Title';
  urgency      : Association to Urgency default 'M';
  status       : Association to Status default 'N';
  conversation : Composition of many {
                   key ID        : UUID;
                       timestamp : type of managed : createdAt;
                       author    : type of managed : createdBy;
                       message   : String;
                 };
}

/**
* Customers entitled to create support Incidents.
*/
entity Customers : managed {
  key ID           : String;
      firstName    : String;
      lastName     : String;
      name         : String = trim(firstName || ' ' || lastName);
      email        : EMailAddress;
      phone        : PhoneNumber;
      incidents    : Association to many Incidents
                       on incidents.customer = $self;
      creditCardNo : String(16) @assert.format: '^[1-9]\d{15}$';
      addresses    : Composition of many Addresses
                       on addresses.customer = $self;
}

entity Addresses : cuid, managed {
  customer      : Association to Customers;
  city          : String;
  postCode      : String;
  streetAddress : String;
}

entity Status : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        new = 'N';
        assigned = 'A';
        in_process = 'I';
        on_hold = 'H';
        resolved = 'R';
        closed = 'C';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

entity Urgency : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        high = 'H';
        medium = 'M';
        low = 'L';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

type EMailAddress : String;
type PhoneNumber  : String;

The model uses the namespace: sap.capire.incidents

It imports three built-in CDS types from @sap/cds/common:

- cuid → automatically generates a UUID as the primary key.
- managed → automatically tracks createdAt, createdBy, modifiedAt, modifiedBy.
- CodeList → a base type for value-list entities like Status and Urgency.

Here is a description of each entity defined in the schema:

INCIDENTS
The core entity of the application. Each incident is raised by a Customer and has a title, an urgency level (default: Medium), and a status (default: New). It also contains an inline Composition of conversations — meaning each incident can have multiple conversation/comment entries stored together with it.

CUSTOMERS
Represents the customers who raise incidents. Stores their first name, last name, email, phone, and credit card number. The "name" field is a computed field that automatically combines firstName and lastName. Each customer can have multiple Addresses and multiple Incidents linked to them.

ADDRESSES
Stores the physical address of a customer, including city, postal code, and street address. It is a composition child of Customers.

STATUS
A code list entity representing the lifecycle stages of an incident:
N → New
A → Assigned
I → In Process
H → On Hold
R → Resolved
C → Closed
Each status has a criticality value used for UI color coding in Fiori.

URGENCY
A code list entity representing the priority level of an incident:
H → High
M → Medium
L → Low
Each urgency level also carries a criticality value for UI color coding.


STEP 3 — CREATE THE SERVICES

CAP uses service definitions to expose your entities as OData endpoints.
We will create two separate service files inside the "srv" folder.

a) incidents_admin.cds

This service is used by administrators to manage customers and incidents. It exposes the Customers entity (with OData Draft enabled for edit flows) and the Incidents entity.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-admin

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';


/**
 * Service used by administrators to manage customers and incidents.
 */

 : 'incidents-admin'
service AdminService {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;
}


b) incidents_processor.cds

This service is used by support personnel (incident processors). It exposes the Incidents entity (with OData Draft enabled) as the main working entity. The Customers entity is auto-exposed as a read-only entity — meaning processors can view customer data when working on an incident, but cannot modify it.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-processor

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';

/**
 * Service used by support personell, i.e. the incidents' 'processors'.
 */


@path: 'incidents-processor'
service ProcessorService  {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;

    // 
    // entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;

    annotate my.Customers with .autoexpose; // Exposes Customers entity as read only entity
}


> WHY TWO SERVICES?
Separating admin and processor services is a best practice in CAP. It lets you assign different authorization roles to each service and expose only what each user role actually needs. This follows the  principle of least privilege and makes it easier to apply security restrictions later (covered in a future part of this series).


STEP 4 — GENERATE CSV TEMPLATES AND ADD INITIAL DATA

CAP provides a built-in command to auto-generate CSV template files based on your domain model.

Run:

cds add data

This creates empty CSV files inside a newly created "db/data" folder.

The file names follow the pattern: <namespace>-<EntityName>.csv
e.g., sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv

CAP automatically picks up these files and loads them as seed/initial data every time the application starts in development mode.

Now add the following sample data to each file:

a) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Addresses.csv
Contains address records for each customer (city, postal code, street).

ID,customer_ID,city,postCode,streetAddress
17e00347-dc7e-4ca9-9c5d-06ccef69f064,1004155,Rome,00164,Piazza Adriana
d8e797d9-6507-4aaa-b43f-5d2301df5135,1004161,Munich,80809,Olympia Park
ff13d2fa-e00f-4ee5-951c-3303f490777b,1004100,Walldorf,69190,Dietmar-Hopp-Allee


b) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv
Contains three sample customers with ID, name, email, and phone.

ID,firstName,lastName,email,phone
1004155,Daniel,Watts,daniel.watts@demo.com,+39-555-123
1004161,Stormy,Weathers,stormy.weathers@demo.com,+49-020-022
1004100,Sunny,Sunshine,sunny.sunshine@demo.com,+49-555-789


c) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.conversation.csv
Contains conversation/comment entries linked to incidents via the
"up__ID" foreign key (CAP convention for composition parent).

ID,up__ID,timestamp,author,message
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1995-12-17T03:24:00Z,Harry John,Can you please check if battery connections are fine?
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd843c,3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1995-12-18T04:24:00Z,Emily Elizabeth,Can you please check if there are any loose connections?
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,2022-09-04T12:00:00Z,Sunny Sunshine,Please check why the solar panel is broken.
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a158cd7,3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,2022-09-04T13:00:00Z,Bradley Flowers,What exactly is wrong?


d) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.csv
Contains four sample incidents with title, urgency code, status code, and a reference to the customer who raised them.

ID,customer_ID,title,urgency_code,status_code
3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1004155,Inverter not functional,H,C
3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1004161,No current on a sunny day,H,N
3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,1004161,Strange noise when switching off Inverter,M,N
3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,1004100,Solar panel broken,H,I


e) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Status.csv
Contains the status code list: N, A, I, H, R, C with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
N,New,0
A,Assigned,0
I,In Process,2
H,On Hold,0
R,Resolved,3
C,Closed,1


f) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Urgency.csv
Contains the urgency code list: H, M, L with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
H,High,1
M,Medium,2
L,Low,3


STEP 5 — RUN THE PROJECT WITH SQLITE

First, deploy the schema to a local SQLite database file:

cds deploy --to sqlite

Then start the development server with live-reload:

cds watch

Once the server starts, open your browser and go to: http://localhost:4004 in your browser.

You should see the CDS welcome page listing all available OData service endpoints:

 

The two services and their exposed entities will be listed:

/odata/v4/incidents-admin
→ Customers (with Fiori preview)
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)

/odata/v4/incidents-processor
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)
→ Customers (with Fiori preview — read-only)

You can click the "Fiori preview" link next to any entity to open an auto-generated Fiori Elements UI — useful for quick testing without building a full UI yet.

You can also directly access the raw OData feed in the browser.

For example: http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents

This will return the incident records from your CSV seed data in JSON format, confirming that the data was loaded correctly.

 


SUMMARY

In Part 1, we successfully:

✔ Created a CAP Node.js project using cds init
✔ Defined the domain model with Incidents, Customers, Addresses, Conversations, Status, and Urgency entities
✔ Created two OData services — AdminService and ProcessorService —with appropriate entity exposure for each user role
✔ Added initial seed data using CSV files
✔ Ran and validated the application locally using SQLite

The application is fully functional locally. In Part 2 , we will replace SQLite with SAP HANA Cloud and prepare the project for cloud deployment.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-2]
entry.content:
Adding CDS Annotations to Build the Fiori UI

In Part 1 , we built and tested our CAP application locally using SQLite. The OData services were working, but when we clicked "Fiori preview" the UI showed raw data with no field labels, no filters, and no meaningful layout.

In this Part, we will add CDS Annotations to shape the Fiori Elements UI —  defining what fields appear, how they are labelled, how the list and detail pages are structured, and which fields get dropdown value helps.

The best part: we do all of this entirely in CDS — no UI5 code needed. Fiori Elements reads these annotations and automatically renders the UI.


WHAT ARE CDS ANNOTATIONS?

CDS Annotations are metadata decorators that you add to your entities or service projections. They tell the Fiori Elements framework how to render the UI — which columns appear in the list, what sections exist on the detail page, which fields should show a value help dropdown, and how labels should read in the browser.

We organize our annotations into three separate files, each focused on one concern:

labels.cds → Human-readable field labels for UI display
layout.cds → Page layout: list columns, detail sections, filters
valuehelps.cds → Dropdown/value help configuration for input fields

Keeping them separate makes the code easier to read and maintain as the application grows.

 

STEP 1 — CREATE THE ANNOTATIONS FOLDER AND FILES

Inside the "app" folder of your CAP project, create a new folder named: annotations

Your folder structure will look like this:

incident-management/
├── app/
│ └── annotations/
│ ├── labels.cds
│ ├── layout.cds
│ └── valuehelps.cds
├── db/
└── srv/

Now create each of the three files with the content below.


a) app/annotations/labels.cds

This file assigns human-readable labels to every field in the Incidents, Customers, Addresses, Status, and Urgency entities. Without these, Fiori Elements falls back to the raw technical field names (e.g., "status_code" instead of "Status").

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../../db/schema';

annotate my.Incidents with @title: 'Incidents' {
    ID           @title          : 'ID';
    createdAt    @title          : 'Created At';
    createdBy    @title          : 'Created By';
    modifiedAt   @title          : 'Modified At';
    modifiedBy   @title          : 'Modified By';
    customer     @title: 'Customer'  : {
        Text           : customer.name,
        TextArrangement: #TextOnly
    };
    title        @title          : 'Title';
    urgency      @title: 'Urgency'   : {
        Text           : urgency.descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    status       @title: 'Status'    : {
        Text           : status.descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    conversation @title          : 'Conversation';
}

annotate my.Customers {
    ID           @title: 'Customer ID';
    createdAt    @title: 'Created At';
    createdBy    @title: 'Created By';
    modifiedAt   @title: 'Modified At';
    modifiedBy   @title: 'Modified By';
    firstName    @title: 'First Name';
    lastName     @title: 'Last Name';
    name         @title: 'Name';
    email        @title: 'Email';
    phone        @title: 'Phone';
    creditCardNo @title: 'Credit Card No';
    incidents    @title: 'Incidents';
    addresses    @title: 'Addresses';
}

annotate my.Addresses with @title: 'Addresses' {
    customer      @title         : 'Customer';
    city          @title         : 'City';
    postCode      @title         : 'Post Code';
    streetAddress @title         : 'Street Address';
}

annotate my.Status {
    code        @title: 'Status Code'  : {
        Text           : descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    criticality @title: 'Criticality';
    descr       @title: 'Status Description';
}

annotate my.Urgency {
    code  @title: 'Urgency Code'  : {
        Text           : descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
}

b) app/annotations/layout.cds
This file defines the full UI layout for the ProcessorService.Incidents entity. It controls both the List Report page and the Object (detail) page.

using {ProcessorService} from '../../srv/incidents_processor';

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents with : {
    HeaderInfo         : {
        TypeName      : 'Incident',
        TypeNamePlural: 'Incidents',
        Title         : {Value: title},
        Description   : {Value: customer.name}
    },
    Facets             : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.CollectionFacet',
            Label : 'Overview',
            Facets: [
                {
                    Label : 'General Information',
                    $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
                    Target: '@UI.FieldGroup#GenInfo',
                },
                {
                    Label : 'Status',
                    $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
                    Target: '@UI.FieldGroup#Status',
                }
            ],

        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            Label : 'Conversations',
            Target: 'conversation/@UI.LineItem',
        }
    ],

    FieldGroup #Status : {
        Label: 'Details',
        $Type: 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {
                Value                    : status_code,
                Label                    : 'Status',
                Criticality              : status.criticality,
                CriticalityRepresentation: #WithIcon,
            },
            {
                Value                    : urgency_code,
                Label                    : 'Urgency',
                Criticality              : urgency.criticality,
                CriticalityRepresentation: #WithoutIcon,
            }
        ]
    },

    FieldGroup #GenInfo: {
        Label: 'Overview',
        $Type: 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {Value: customer.name},
            {Value: title}
        ]
    },

    LineItem           : [
        {
            Value         : title,
            .Importance: #High
        },
        {
            Value         : customer.name,
            .Importance: #High,
        },
        {
            Value                    : status_code,
            Criticality              : status.criticality,
            CriticalityRepresentation: #WithIcon,
            .Importance           : #High,
        },
        {
            Value                    : urgency_code,
            .Importance           : #Low,
            Criticality              : urgency.criticality,
            CriticalityRepresentation: #WithoutIcon,
        },
    ],

    SelectionFields    : [
        customer_ID,
        status_code,
        urgency_code
    ],
};

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents.conversation with : {LineItem: [
    {
        Value: message,
        Label: 'Message'
    },
    {Value: author},
    {Value: timestamp}
], }


c) app/annotations/valuehelps.cds

This file adds dropdown / value help popups to input fields on the Incidents entity, making data entry easier and consistent.

using {ProcessorService} from '../../srv/incidents_processor';

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents with {
    status   .ValueListWithFixedValues;
    urgency  .ValueListWithFixedValues;
    customer .ValueList: {
        CollectionPath: 'Customers',
        Parameters    : [
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
                LocalDataProperty: customer_ID,
                ValueListProperty: 'ID'
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'name',
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'email',
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'phone',
            },
        ]
    }
}


STEP 2 — RUN LOCALLY WITH SQLITE AND PREVIEW THE UI

Now that annotations are in place, re-deploy the schema and restart the server:

cds deploy --to sqlite
cds watch

Open your browser at: http://localhost:4004

You will see the familiar CDS welcome page. This time, click "Fiori preview" next to the "Incidents" entity under the /odata/v4/processor service.

 

The List Report and Object page now renders with full layout and labels.

 

 


SUMMARY

In Part 2, we successfully:

✔ Created an "annotations" folder under app/ with three CDS files
✔ Added field labels for all entities (labels.cds)
✔ Defined the List Report columns, filter bar, Object Page layout and detail page sections (layout.cds)
✔ Configured value help dropdowns for Status, Urgency, and Customer (valuehelps.cds)
✔ Previewed the fully rendered Fiori Elements UI locally with SQLite

In Part 3 , we will set up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP — creating the database instance and mapping it to our Cloud Foundry space.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-3]
entry.content:
Setting Up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP

In Part 2 , we added CDS Annotations and previewed the Fiori UI locally with SQLite. Now it is time to move to the cloud database layer.

In this Part, we will set up SAP HANA Cloud on SAP BTP so our CAP application can use it as the production database. This involves several one-time BTP configuration steps before we can deploy anything.

Here is what we will do in this part:

1. Verify SAP HANA Cloud is entitled at the Global Account level
2. Assign the HANA Cloud service plans to your Subaccount
3. Subscribe to HANA Cloud Tools via the Service Marketplace
4. Assign the required HANA Cloud roles to your user
5. Create a SAP HANA Cloud database instance
6. Map the Cloud Foundry environment to the HANA Cloud instance

All of these steps are done in the SAP BTP Cockpit and SAP HANA Cloud Central.

STEP 1 — VERIFY HANA CLOUD ENTITLEMENTS IN THE GLOBAL ACCOUNT

Before creating any HANA Cloud resources, confirm that your Global Account has the SAP HANA Cloud service entitlement available.

In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to:

Global Account → Entitlements → Service Assignments

In the search box, type "HANA" to filter the services.

 You should see the services available.

 

If you see these listed, your Global Account is entitled.

Proceed to Step 2.

NOTE: If SAP HANA Cloud does not appear here, contact your BTP Global Account administrator to enable the entitlement.


STEP 2 — ASSIGN HANA CLOUD SERVICE PLANS TO YOUR SUBACCOUNT

Entitlements available at the Global Account level must be explicitly assigned to a Subaccount before they can be used there.

Navigate to:

Global Account → Entitlements → Entity Assignments

Filter by your subaccount (e.g., "trial") and locate the SAP HANA Cloud service. Click "Edit" and add the required service plans to your subaccount.

- Search for "hana" in the services list on the left
- Select "SAP HANA Cloud" from the results
- On the right panel, check the following plans and Click "Add Service Plans" to confirm

 

After saving, the Entity Assignments page will show SAP HANA Cloud with the assigned plans for your subaccount.

 

STEP 3 — SUBSCRIBE TO HANA CLOUD TOOLS IN THE SERVICE MARKETPLACE

SAP HANA Cloud is managed through a browser-based graphical tool called SAP HANA Cloud Central. To access it, you must first subscribe to the "hana-cloud-tools" application in your subaccount's Service Marketplace.

Navigate to your Subaccount, then go to: Services → Service Marketplace

Search for "HANA CLOUD". Click on the "SAP HANA Cloud" tile.

 

 

In the right panel, click "Create". A wizard dialog will open.

 

 Click "Create". This creates a subscription to HANA Cloud Central.

 

Navigate to:  Services → Instances and Subscriptions

You should now see "SAP HANA Cloud" listed under Subscriptions with Status: "Subscribed".

 

STEP 4 — ASSIGN HANA CLOUD ROLES TO YOUR USER

Before you can open HANA Cloud Central, your BTP user needs the correct role collections assigned. Without them, you will see a "Not authorized" error when trying to access HANA Cloud Central.

 

 

 

The required SAP HANA Cloud role collections are:
https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud/sap-hana-cloud-administration-guide/role-collections-for-sap-hana-cloud

 

To assign these roles, navigate to your Subaccount:

 

Security → Users

Click on your user  to open the details panel.
Click "Assign Role Collection".

 

 

In the "Assign Role Collection" dialog, search for "SAP HANA CLOUD".
Select all the required role collections:

 

Click "Assign Role Collection" to save.

 

STEP 5 — CREATE THE SAP HANA CLOUD DATABASE INSTANCE

Now open SAP HANA Cloud Central. You can access it from:

Services → Instances and Subscriptions → SAP HANA Cloud → "Go to Application"

HANA Cloud Central will open in a new browser tab. The Instances page will show "No instances" — we need to create one.

 

Click "Create Instance". A multi-step wizard opens with 8 steps.

1

 

2

 

3

 

4

 

5

 

6

 

7

 

8

 

After creation, HANA Cloud Central will show both instances as "Running":

 

STEP 6 — MAP THE CLOUD FOUNDRY ENVIRONMENT TO THE HANA CLOUD INSTANCE

The HANA Cloud instance was created but it needs to be mapped to your Cloud Foundry organization and space. This mapping is what enables CAP to create HDI (HANA Deployment Infrastructure) containers for schema deployment when you run cds deploy --to hana.

Click on the "SHC_DB" instance to open its details. 

 

Click "Manage Configuration" → go to the "Instance Mapping" tab.

Click "Add Mapping". You will be redirected to sign into the CF environment.

 

Sign in with your default identity provider credentials. After authentication, return to the Instance Mapping tab and click "Add Mapping" again.

 

Now a new row appears. Fill in the mapping:

Click "Review and Save" to confirm the mapping. The HANA Cloud instance is now connected to your Cloud Foundry space and ready to receive HDI container deployments from the CAP application.

 


SUMMARY

In Part 3, we successfully:

✔ Verified SAP HANA Cloud entitlements at the Global Account level
✔ Assigned HANA Cloud service plans to the trial subaccount
✔ Subscribed to SAP HANA Cloud Tools (hana-cloud-tools) via Service Marketplace
✔ Assigned all required SAP HANA Cloud role collections to our user
✔ Created a SAP HANA Cloud database instance (SHC_DB) with a linked Data Lake (SHC_DL) using the free tier
✔ Mapped the Cloud Foundry organization and space to the HANA instance to enable HDI container creation

The SAP HANA Cloud infrastructure is now fully set up and ready. In Part 4, we will connect the CAP application to this HANA Cloud instance and deploy the backend to Cloud Foundry.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-4]
entry.content:
Connecting CAP application to HANA Cloud and Deploying  to Cloud Foundry

In Part 3 , we created the SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP and mapped it to our Cloud Foundry space.

In this Part, we will:

- Update package.json with the correct CDS profiles for each environment
- Run cds add hana to wire the CAP project for HANA Cloud deployment
- Build the MTA archive using mbt build
- Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
- Verify the deployment and explore the HANA tables in DB Explorer
- Run the app locally in hybrid mode — connected to the deployed HANA instance

PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed before proceeding:

- Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — logged in to your BTP CF environment: cf login
- MBT (Multi-Target Application Build Tool): npm install -g mbt
- @sap/cds-dk (already installed from Part 1)


STEP 1 — UPDATE PACKAGE.JSON WITH CDS PROFILES

The package.json in the CAP project already has a "cds" section that defines how the app connects to the database in each environment. We need to make sure the three profiles are correctly configured:

development → uses SQLite (local, in-file database — for cds watch)
hybrid → uses SAP HANA Cloud (local app, cloud database — for testing)
production → uses SAP HANA Cloud (fully deployed on CF)

Update or verify the "cds" section in your package.json:

{
  "name": "incident-management",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Incident Management CAP Application",
  "repository": "<Add your repository here>",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "private": true,
  "dependencies": {
    "@cap-js/hana": "^2",
    "@sap/cds": "^9",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "@sap/xssec": "^4",
    "express": "^5"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cap-js/sqlite": "^2",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "cds-plugin-ui5": "^0.17.0"
  },
  "cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[development]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "sqlite"
        }
      },
      "[hybrid]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      },
      "[production]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "watch": "cds watch",
    "watch-incidents": "cds watch --open incidents/index.html?sap-ui-xx-viewCache=false --livereload false"
  }
}

WHY THREE PROFILES?
This pattern lets you use the same codebase across all environments without changing any code — just switch the profile.

SQLite keeps local development fast and offline.

Hybrid lets you test against real HANA data without a full CF deployment.

Production is used when the app runs on Cloud Foundry.

 

STEP 2 — RUN cds add hana

Run the following command in the terminal from inside the project folder:

cds add hana

This single command does several important things to prepare your CAP project for SAP HANA Cloud deployment:

- Adds the @cap-js/hana dependency to package.json (HANA database adapter)
- Generates or updates the mta.yaml file with the correct MTA module definitions for CF deployment
- Adds an HDI container resource definition to mta.yaml
- Configures the production CDS profile to use hana-cloud as the db kind

After running this command, your mta.yaml will define three components:

1. incident-management-srv (Node.js module — the CAP OData service)
- Deployed as a CF application
- Provides: srv-api (the service URL consumed by the Fiori app later)
- Requires: incident-management-db (the HDI container)

2. incident-management-db-deployer (hdb module — the schema deployer)
- A one-time deployer app that pushes the CDS schema to HANA
- Runs during deployment then stops (Stopped state is normal)
- Requires: incident-management-db

3. incident-management-db (resource — the HDI container)
- Type: com.sap.xs.hdi-container
- Service: hana, Plan: hdi-shared
- This is the actual schema container created inside your HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) that was set up in Part 3 


STEP 3 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the MBT build command to compile the project into a deployable archive:

mbt build

This command:
- Reads the mta.yaml configuration
- Runs `npm install` and `npx cds build --production` to compile the CDS model
- Packages all modules into a single .mtar archive file

The output archive is created at:

mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

 

STEP 4 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Make sure you are logged in to CF and targeting the correct space:

cf login -a <api endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s dev

Now deploy the MTA archive:

cf deploy mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

CF will:
1. Create the HDI container service instance (incident-management-db)
2. Deploy and run the db-deployer to push the schema to HANA
3. Start the CAP service application (incident-management-srv)

Once deployment completes, navigate to your CF space in BTP Cockpit:

Trial Home →   f030ff54trial   → trial   → dev → Applications

You will see two applications in the "dev" space:

 

STEP 5 — VERIFY THE HDI CONTAINER INSTANCE

Navigate to:

Space: dev → Services → Instances

You should see the HDI container service instance that was automatically created during deployment:

 This HDI container lives inside the SHC_DB HANA Cloud instance we created in Part 3. CAP automatically created it during the cf deploy step.

 

STEP 6 — EXPLORE THE HANA TABLES IN DB EXPLORER

Now let's verify the schema was deployed correctly by exploring the tables in SAP HANA Database Explorer.

Open HANA Cloud Central → click "Open" on the SHC_DB instance →
select "SAP HANA Database Explorer".

In Database Explorer, the left panel shows your SHC-DB instance connected as DBADMIN. To view the HDI container tables, click the "+" (Add Instance) icon.

 

Select "HDI Container" as the Instance Type, filter by your org (f030ff54trial) and space (dev).

Select "incident-management-db" and click OK.

 

The HDI container is now added to the left panel. Expand it to see the Tables folder. You will find all the CDS entities have been deployed as HANA tables.

Click on SAP_CAPIRE_INCIDENTS_INCIDENTS to inspect its structure:


STEP 7 — RUNNING IN HYBRID MODE

Now that the CAP app is deployed and the HDI container exists in BTP, you can run your local development server connected directly to the cloud HANA database. This is the "hybrid" mode — your app runs locally but reads and writes real HANA Cloud data.

This is very useful for testing local code changes against production data before deploying again.

Step 1 — Bind your local project to the HDI container service instance:

cds bind -2 incident-management-db:key

What this does:
- Connects to Cloud Foundry and retrieves the service key for the "incident-management-db" HDI container instance
- Creates a service key on BTP (if one doesn't exist)
- Saves the HANA connection credentials (host, schema, user, password) into .cdsrc-private.json under the "hybrid" profile
- The .cdsrc-private.json file is local only — never commit it to git

Step 2 — Start the server in hybrid mode:

cds watch --profile hybrid

The terminal confirms the app is now connected to the real HANA Cloud database, showing:
- The HANA host and schema ID
- Driver: com.sap.db.jdbc.Driver (JDBC connection to HANA)
- Both ProcessorService and AdminService are being served

Your app on localhost:4004 now reads and writes directly to the HANA Cloud instance in BTP — the Fiori preview will show and save real data.


SUMMARY

In Part 4, we successfully:

✔ Configured package.json with three CDS profiles: development (SQLite), hybrid (local + HANA Cloud), production (CF + HANA)
✔ Ran cds add hana to wire the project for HANA Cloud and generate mta.yaml
✔ Built the deployable MTA archive using mbt build
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
✔ Verified the two CF apps: incident-management-srv (Started) and incident-management-db-deployer (Stopped — expected)
✔ Confirmed the HDI container instance was created with 2 bindings
✔ Explored the HANA tables and seed data in SAP HANA Database Explorer
✔ Connected the local app to the HANA Cloud instance using hybrid mode

The CAP backend is now live on Cloud Foundry and backed by SAP HANA Cloud.
In Part 5 , we will build the Fiori Elements UI application and connect it to this deployed CAP service.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-6]
entry.content:
Building and Deploying the Fiori App to HTML5 Repository

In the previous parts we:

✔ Built and tested the CAP backend locally (Part 1  & Part 2 )
✔ Set up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP (Part 3 )
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry (Part 4 )
✔ Configured the BTP Destination to point to the CAP service (Part 5 )

In this Part, we will:
- Generate the Fiori Elements app using the SAP Fiori Application Generator
- Add deployment configuration for Cloud Foundry and the HTML5 Repository
- Build and deploy the Fiori app as a standalone HTML5 application
- Access the fully working end-to-end application via SAP Build Work Zone

 

PREREQUISITES

Before proceeding, make sure the following are in place:

- VS Code with the SAP Fiori Tools Extension Pack installed / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Cloud Foundry CLI logged in (cf login)
- The CAP backend deployed and running (from Part 4)
- The destination "destination-trial" configured in BTP (from Part 5)

> IMPORTANT — You also need the following active subscriptions in your BTP Subaccount before you can access the deployed Fiori app:
> - SAP HTML5 Application Repository Service
> - SAP Cloud Identity Services
> - SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (for the Launchpad)

> Follow the step-by-step guide here  to enable these.

 

PART A — DEVELOPING THE FIORI UI APP

STEP 1 — OPEN THE SAP FIORI APPLICATION GENERATOR

In VS Code/BAS, press Ctrl+Shift+P and search for: SAP Fiori: Open Application Generator

The SAP Fiori Generator panel opens with a multi-step wizard on the left.

 

STEP 2 — SELECT THE TEMPLATE

On the Template Selection screen, choose: List Report Page

This template generates a two-page Fiori Elements app — a List Report for browsing records and an Object Page for viewing/editing individual records.
It uses the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 to render the UI automatically.

 Click "Next >".

 

STEP 3 — DATA SOURCE AND SERVICE SELECTION

Data Source:  Connect to an OData Service

 Click "Next >". The generator fetches the metadata from the live service.

STEP 4 — ENTITY SELECTION

Once the metadata is loaded, select:

Main Entity: Incidents
Navigation Entity: None
Generate Annotations: Yes
Table Type: Responsive

The generator will pick up the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 automatically since "Generate Annotations" is set to Yes.

Click "Next >".

STEP 5 — PROJECT ATTRIBUTES

Fill in the project details:

 Click "Finish". The generator scaffolds the complete Fiori Elements project in the specified folder. This creates the "incidentmanagement" app folder with webapp/, manifest.json, xs-app.json, and all config files.

STEP 6 — RUN AND TEST APP LOCALLY

npm run start-noflp

 

PART B — DEPLOYING THE FIORI APP TO HTML5 REPOSITORY

STEP 7 — ADD DEPLOYMENT CONFIGURATION

Navigate into the Fiori app folder and run:

npm run deploy-config

This command launches an interactive prompt to configure the deployment.


Answer the prompts as follows:

Target environment: Cloud Foundry
Destination name: destination-trial
App Router: Managed Application Router

Running this command generates/updates two key files:

mta.yaml → Multi-Target Application descriptor for CF deployment.
Defines three CF resources to be created:
- incidentmanagement-destination-service (destination, lite)→ includes a built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 resources
- incidentmanagement-uaa (XSUAA, application plan)→ OAuth2 security for the app, configured via xs-security.json
- incidentmanagement-repo-host (html5-apps-repo, app-host)→ the HTML5 Application Repository bucket that stores the app

ui5-deploy.yaml → UI5 build configuration.
Packages the app into incidentmanagement.zip (excluding test/ and localService/ folders) and bundles xs-app.json into the archive for AppRouter routing rules.

 

STEP 8 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the build command:

npm run build:mta

This command:
- Runs npm install and npm run build:cf to compile the UI5 application
- Packages the compiled app into incidentmanagement.zip
- Bundles everything into a deployable MTA archive: mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

 

STEP 9 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Deploy the Fiori app MTA archive to Cloud Foundry:

cf deploy mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

During deployment, CF will automatically:
1. Create the XSUAA service instance (incidentmanagement-xsuaa-service)
2. Create the HTML5 App Repository host (incidentmanagement-html5-service)
3. Create the Destination service instance (incidentmanagement-destination-service)— including the built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 CDN resources
4. Uploads the Fiori app zip to the HTML5 Repository
5. Register the app's destination content (html5 repo host + XSUAA token exchange)

STEP 10 — VERIFY AND ACCESS LIVE APPLICATION IN HTML5 REPO

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Subaccount and click:

HTML5 Applications (in the left navigation)

You will see the deployed Fiori app listed under the Managed Application Router provided by SAP Build Work Zone:

 

Click on "incidentmanagement" in the HTML5 Applications page to open the app in SAP Build Work Zone Launchpad.

The Fiori Elements List Report opens in the browser — now running fully in the cloud, reading live data from SAP HANA Cloud via the CAP service:

 
SERIES SUMMARY:

Over 6 parts, we built and deployed a complete, production-grade Incident Management application on SAP BTP using modern SAP technologies:

Part 1  → CAP Node.js project with domain model, OData services,CSV seed data — running locally on SQLite

Part 2  → CDS Annotations for the Fiori Elements UI:field labels, list/object page layout, value helps

Part 3  → SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP — entitlements,roles, instance creation, CF environment mapping

Part 4  → CAP backend deployed to Cloud Foundry, HDI containercreated, schema deployed, hybrid mode with cds bind

Part 5  → BTP Destination (destination-trial) configured to bridge the Fiori app to the CAP service URL

Part 6 (This Part) → Fiori Elements app generated, configured for HTML5 Repo, deployed to Cloud Foundry — live end-to-end on BTP

The result is a fully decoupled architecture where the CAP backend and the Fiori frontend are deployed and scaled independently a pattern recommended by SAP for modern BTP applications.

I hope this series helped you understand how CAP, HANA Cloud, Fiori Elements, and BTP services come together to build a real application.

 In the coming parts, we will get to know about configuring the App Router, understanding xs-security.json, adding validations, implementing custom actions, securing our app with XSUAA, and even more. Stay tuned!
